questions.question: What is the accelerate-go distance?

options.text: The runway needed to accelerate to rotation speed and stop after an engine failure
The horizontal distance to accelerate, lose an engine, and continue to 50 feet AGL on the remaining engine
The total distance needed to take off and reach cruise altitude on both engines
The distance needed to abort a takeoff from any speed during the roll

questions.explanation: Accelerate-go distance is the total horizontal distance required to accelerate to VR or VLOF, experience an engine failure, and continue the takeoff climbing to 50 feet AGL on the remaining engine.

questions.question: What rate of climb defines the single-engine service ceiling?

options.text: 100 feet per minute
0 feet per minute
200 feet per minute
50 feet per minute

questions.explanation: The single-engine service ceiling is the highest altitude where the airplane can maintain a steady climb of 50 fpm with one engine at full power and the dead engine's propeller feathered.

questions.question: You are cruising at 10,000 feet and your single-engine absolute ceiling is 6,000 feet. What happens if you lose an engine?

options.text: The airplane maintains altitude if you fly at VYSE
You must immediately descend to pattern altitude
The airplane drifts down to 6,000 feet, the highest altitude it can sustain on one engine
You can maintain 10,000 feet by increasing power on the operating engine

questions.explanation: Above the single-engine absolute ceiling, the airplane cannot maintain altitude on one engine and will gradually drift down until leveling off at the single-engine absolute ceiling.

questions.question: Does the FAA require light twins (6,000 lbs or less) to have a positive single-engine climb rate?

options.text: Yes, at least 100 fpm at sea level
Yes, at least 50 fpm at 5,000 feet
No, the regulation only requires that the OEI climb rate be determined, not that it be positive
No, but positive climb is required above 3,000 feet MSL

questions.explanation: Under 14 CFR Part 23.67, for light twins with a maximum weight of 6,000 lbs or less and VSO of 61 knots or less, the OEI climb rate must only be determined — it can legally be a negative number.

questions.question: How does a headwind affect climb gradient?

options.text: It has no effect on climb gradient
It degrades climb gradient because the airplane flies slower
It improves climb gradient because less ground distance is covered for the same altitude gain
It improves climb gradient only above the single-engine service ceiling

questions.explanation: Unlike rate of climb, climb gradient is affected by wind. A headwind improves gradient because you cover less ground distance for the same altitude gained.
